title: Banned Antibiotic Residues Trigger Shrimp Export Rejections; CDSCO Seeks State Action
summary: New Delhi: Prompted by a rise in shrimp export rejections linked to residues of banned antibiotics, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO) has directed all State and Union Territory...
thbAction: Use the article as an entry point to pitch THB's commercial visibility layer for territory planning, account prioritization, and next-best-action for pharma leadership teams.
contentHtml:
New Delhi: Prompted by a rise in shrimp export rejections linked to residues of banned antibiotics, the Central Drugs Standard Control Organization (CDSCO) has directed all State and Union Territory Drug Controllers to furnish details of inspections, enforcement actions, and implementation measures related to the ban on chloramphenicol and nitrofuran drugs in food-producing animals.

In a letter issued on June 3, 2026, the Drugs Controller General (India) (DCGI) referred to a notification dated March 11, 2025, which prohibited the import, manufacture, sale, distribution, and use of chloramphenicol and nitrofuran drugs and their formulations in any food-producing animal rearing system.

Regarding the above, earlier the Medical Dialogues Team reported that in exercise of powers conferred by section 26A of the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, and after consultation with the Drugs Technical Advisory Board, the Central Government prohibited the import, manufacture, sale and distribution of the following drugs, for use in any food-producing animal rearing system, with immediate effect, namely

"(1) Chloramphenicol and its formulations; and

(2) Nitrofuran and its formulations."

Despite the prohibition, the Marine Products Export Development Authority (MPEDA) has reported that residues of these banned antibiotics continue to be detected in shrimp export consignments.

According to information shared by MPEDA, 43 per cent of shrimp export rejections by major importing countries, including the European Union, the United States, and Japan during 2025, were linked to the presence of chloramphenicol and nitrofuran residues.

The affected cases involved more than 40 aquaculture farms across multiple states. Andhra Pradesh accounted for 74 per cent of the implicated farms, followed by Odisha (13 per cent), West Bengal (8.7 per cent), and Gujarat (4.3 per cent).

Expressing concern over the continued detection of banned antibiotic residues despite the regulatory prohibition, CDSCO has sought details from state regulators regarding the implementation of the ban, inspections conducted at veterinary drug establishments, and enforcement action taken against violators.

CDSCO has specifically sought the following information from states and UTs:

  1. The current mechanism for implementation of the notification across the states and UTs.
  2. The number of inspections conducted at veterinary medical shops and related establishments, along with their outcomes.
  3. Details of punitive actions initiated or completed against violators, if any, as part of enforcement efforts.

The regulator has also instructed states and UTs to ensure that the above-mentioned drugs and their formulations are sold only to licensed manufacturers (bulk and formulation) through licensed premises and strictly for the intended purpose, with proper reconciliation. States have also been directed to initiate appropriate regulatory action in cases of violations under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act, 1940, and the Drugs Rules, 1945.

CDSCO has requested all states and UTs to furnish the required information in a time-bound manner to facilitate further action in the matter.
